title: Exposure Device Labeling and Use
narrationScript: An exposure device spends its working life in truck beds and storage cages, so it has to identify itself to anyone who finds it. Federal rules require a durable, legible label, attached by the user, carrying five facts: the radionuclide's chemical symbol and mass number, the activity and the date it was last measured, the sealed source model and serial number, the source manufacturer, and the licensee's name, address, and telephone number. Every container also wears the yellow trefoil with the words caution, radioactive material, and the source assembly itself carries a tag reading danger, radioactive. Use is just as scripted. The device stays locked, key out, except under direct surveillance. A guide tube and exposure head are mandatory whenever the source leaves the shield, and two qualified people staff every field job. Crank smoothly, stay behind your distance and shielding, and after every single exposure, survey the entire device and the full length of the guide tube before anyone touches film. The crank can lie. Only the meter proves the source is home.

sections.heading: The Label Is the Device's Voice
sections.body:
A loaded exposure device spends its working life in truck beds, on scaffold decks, and in storage cages — places where the crew that knows what it is may be nowhere in sight. Labeling is how the device identifies itself to everyone else: the pipefitter who finds it behind a weld shack, the inspector auditing the program, the firefighter working a highway accident with a projector in the wreckage.

10 CFR 34.20 requires the **user** — not just the manufacturer — to keep a **durable, legible, clearly visible label** attached to every radiographic exposure device, bearing five facts:

- **Chemical symbol and mass number** of the radionuclide inside — Ir-192, Co-60, Se-75
- **Activity, and the date that activity was last measured** — the assay date that lets anyone decay-correct the number (a 100-curie Ir-192 source assayed 74 days ago is roughly a 50-curie source today)
- **Model (or product code) and serial number of the sealed source**
- **Manufacturer of the sealed source**
- **Licensee's name, address, and telephone number**

Each line has a job. Radionuclide plus activity lets a responder estimate dose rates if the shield is ever breached. The source serial number ties the device to its leak-test certificate and to the quarterly physical inventory required by 10 CFR 34.29. The licensee contact line is how a lost or stolen device finds its way back to the people legally responsible for it. And because the label describes the **source**, every source exchange is also a label exchange — the plate that arrives with the new source goes on before the device goes back to work.

sections.heading: Warning Markings Everyone Can Read
sections.body:
The identity label speaks to professionals; the **hazard markings** speak to everyone. Under 10 CFR 20.1904, each container of licensed material — exposure device, storage container, source changer — bears the **radiation symbol** and the words **CAUTION, RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL** or **DANGER, RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL**. The trefoil itself is standardized by 10 CFR 20.1901: **magenta, purple, or black on a yellow background**. The standardization is the point — the symbol is recognized at a glance by people who will never read a regulation.

One more warning sits deeper in the system. 10 CFR 34.20 requires every sealed source or **source assembly (pigtail)** to carry — attached or engraved — a durable, legible, visible label reading **DANGER—RADIOACTIVE**, positioned so it cannot interfere with safe operation of the device. It is the last warning available to someone close enough to the source to read it, and it marks the one component that must never be handled directly.

Two more layers complete the picture. The **manufacturer's data plate** carries the device's own make, model, and serial number — the identity that links it to ANSI N432-1980, the design and construction standard 10 CFR Part 34 incorporates by reference, and to your daily inspection and utilization records. And because most projectors double as their own shipping containers, a device used that way must qualify as a **Type B package** under 10 CFR Part 71; the DOT labels and placards it wears on the road are a separate layer, covered with storage and shipment in Module RS-7.

sections.caption: Required markings on a gamma radiography exposure device: the user-applied identity label, the trefoil hazard plate, the keyed lock, the safety plug, and the DANGER—RADIOACTIVE tag on the source assembly.
Every marking on and around an exposure device, and the requirement behind it.

CAUTION (or DANGER), RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL — magenta, purple, or black trefoil on yellow

sections.caption: Pre-shot label walkaround on a loaded projector. Three of these markings are compliant; one fails 10 CFR 34.20 — the exercise below asks you to find it.

sections.heading: Use Begins Before the Source Moves
sections.body:
Proper use starts with custody. A radiographic exposure device is **kept locked — key removed** — except when it is under the **direct surveillance** of a radiographer or a radiographer's assistant; 10 CFR 34.23 allows no third state. And at any location other than a permanent radiographic installation, **two qualified individuals must be present** — the radiographer plus another radiographer or a trained assistant — because radiography may not be performed if only one qualified individual is on site (10 CFR 34.41).

The hookup follows the same sequence every time, after the daily equipment inspection covered in the last lesson:

- **Connect the drive cable to the source assembly and verify positive engagement** — inspect the coupling, then pull-test it. A mis-latched connector is how sources end up disconnected in the guide tube.
- **Attach the guide tube.** Guide tubes are mandatory whenever the source is moved out of the device — Part 34 does not permit projecting a source through open air (10 CFR 34.20).
- **Install the exposure head on the outermost end of the guide tube.** Its job is to stop the source assembly from passing out the end of the tube, and it is required during radiographic operations.
- **Lay the tube out with sweeping bends — never kinks** — and secure the exposure head at the shot position. A kinked tube can trap the source somewhere between you and shielded.
- **Set the crank position for distance and shielding**, out of the primary beam path, before the boundary goes up.

Then the area: post it conspicuously with the radiation-area and high-radiation-area signs required by 10 CFR 20.1902 — the posting exemptions available to other licensees do not apply to industrial radiography (10 CFR 34.53) — and maintain **continuous direct visual surveillance** of the operation to keep unauthorized people out of the high radiation area (10 CFR 34.51).

sections.heading: Driving the Source Out — and Proving It Came Home
sections.body:
With the boundary posted and your partner watching, **crank the source out smoothly and briskly**. Source-transfer time is the highest-dose-rate part of a routine exposure — the source is moving through a lightly shielded guide tube — so hesitation at the crank costs millirem. Stay at the crank position behind your planned distance and shielding, alarming ratemeter on, and never stand over the device or straddle the guide tube while the source is in motion.

Bringing it home is where the discipline is written into regulation:

- **Retract with steady, continuous cranking until the source seats.** The device must **automatically secure** the source assembly when it reaches the fully shielded position, releasable only by a deliberate operation on the device (10 CFR 34.20). Check the lock indicator.
- **Survey — after every exposure, before anything else.** 10 CFR 34.49 requires a survey of the exposure device **and** the guide tube after each exposure, confirming the source is fully shielded **before** exchanging film, repositioning the exposure head, or dismantling equipment. Approach meter-first and walk the full circumference of the device, then the entire guide tube out to the exposure head.
- **Secure and lock every time.** The source assembly is secured in the shielded position each time it returns there, and the device is locked whenever it is not under direct surveillance (10 CFR 34.23).
- **Close out the job.** Install the safety plugs and covers on the outlet fitting, lock box, and drive-cable fitting — they keep water, mud, and sand out of the source path during storage and transport (10 CFR 34.20) — and survey the device again when it is placed into storage (10 CFR 34.49).

sections.title: The crank lies. The meter tells the truth.
sections.body: If the pigtail parts from the drive cable, the cable cranks home with normal feel, the turn count comes out right, and the device may even lock — while the source sits in the exposure head a few steps away. An unshielded 100-curie Ir-192 source reads roughly **50 R/hr at one meter**, and a hand on that exposure head can take a career-ending dose in minutes. Disconnects exactly like this are behind some of the worst injuries in industrial radiography. The post-exposure survey — full device circumference, every foot of guide tube — is the only step in the sequence that catches it. **No reading, no approach.**

sections.caption: The non-negotiable close-out after every exposure: retract, confirm the automatic lock, survey the device and the entire guide tube, then lock, plug, and store — no film exchange until the meter says shielded.

figures.title: After every exposure: the close-out sequence

interactive.title: Spot the Labeling Violation
interactive.intro: It is 6:40 a.m. and the projector just came off the truck for the first shot of the day. Walk the markings the way an inspector would: the trefoil hazard plate, the user-applied source label, the pigtail tag, and the licensee plate.
interactive.prompt: One of these four markings fails the labeling requirements of 10 CFR 34.20. Click it.

regions.label: Trefoil hazard plate
regions.feedback: Compliant. A container of licensed material must bear the radiation symbol and the words CAUTION (or DANGER), RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL — a magenta, purple, or black trefoil on yellow (10 CFR 20.1904 and 20.1901). This plate does its job.

regions.label: Source identity label
regions.feedback: Correct — this is the violation. The user-applied label must be durable and legible, and it must state the activity and the date that activity was last measured. With those worn off, this device does not work today: restore the data from the source certificate and utilization records before the first exposure.

regions.label: Source assembly (pigtail) tag
regions.feedback: Compliant. Every sealed source or source assembly must carry — attached or engraved — a durable, legible, visible label reading DANGER—RADIOACTIVE, placed so it cannot interfere with safe operation (10 CFR 34.20).

regions.label: Licensee plate
regions.feedback: Compliant. The licensee's name, address, and telephone number are required on the device — it is how a lost, stolen, or wrecked projector finds its way back to the people legally responsible for it.

checkQuestions.question: Per 10 CFR 34.20, which of the following is NOT required on the user-applied label of a radiographic exposure device?
options: The chemical symbol and mass number of the radionuclide
The activity and the date on which it was last measured
The model and serial number of the sealed source
The name of the radiographer assigned to the device
checkQuestions.explanation: The label identifies the source and the licensee — including the source manufacturer and the licensee's name, address, and telephone number. No rule puts individual radiographers' names on the device; people are tracked through utilization logs and dosimetry records, not the label.

checkQuestions.question: Which marking must be attached to or engraved on the sealed source assembly (pigtail) itself?
options: DANGER—RADIOACTIVE
CAUTION, HIGH RADIATION AREA
The transport index and package category
The licensee's telephone number
checkQuestions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.20 requires every sealed source or source assembly to carry a durable, legible, visible DANGER—RADIOACTIVE label, placed so it cannot interfere with safe operation — the last warning available to anyone close enough to the source to read it.

checkQuestions.question: An exposure just ended and the source has been cranked back. Under 10 CFR 34.49, before anyone exchanges film, repositions the exposure head, or dismantles equipment, the radiographer must:
options: log the exposure time in the utilization record
re-zero the pocket dosimeters at the crank position
survey the exposure device and the guide tube to confirm the source is fully shielded
count crank turns and verify the retract resistance felt normal
checkQuestions.explanation: The post-exposure survey of the device and the guide tube is the only acceptable proof the source is home. Crank feel and turn counts are hints — a disconnected source defeats both — and paperwork comes after the area is proven safe.

checkQuestions.question: Under 10 CFR 34.23, a loaded radiographic exposure device may be left unlocked only when:
options: it is inside a posted high radiation area
it is under the direct surveillance of a radiographer or a radiographer's assistant
the key is stored within arm's reach of the device
its source has decayed below two curies
checkQuestions.explanation: Locking has exactly one alternative: direct surveillance by qualified personnel. Posting, key placement, and source strength change nothing — the device is either watched or locked with the key removed.

checkQuestions.question: When the source must travel out of the device for an exposure, 10 CFR 34.20 requires:
options: a collimator on every exposure regardless of geometry
projection without a guide tube when the source-to-film distance is short
two independent drive cables connected to the source assembly
a guide tube, with an exposure head on its outermost end to stop the source assembly from passing out of the tube
checkQuestions.explanation: Guide tubes are mandatory whenever the source moves out of the device, and the exposure head on the outermost end is what prevents the source assembly from traveling out the end of the tube. Collimators are sound ALARA practice — covered in the next lesson — but they are not required hardware on every shot.

references: 10 CFR Part 34 — Licenses for Industrial Radiography and Radiation Safety Requirements for Industrial Radiographic Operations (§§34.20, 34.23, 34.41, 34.49, 34.51, 34.53)
10 CFR Part 20 — Standards for Protection Against Radiation (§§20.1901–20.1904, radiation symbol and container labeling)
ANSI N432-1980, Radiological Safety for the Design and Construction of Apparatus for Gamma Radiography
CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §9.3–9.4 (exposure devices: labeling and use)
